prompt:
Ticket from the fulfillment-tools team. Our shipment lookups are linear scans over a slice and the ops dashboard now does thousands per refresh. I want a small in-memory record heap with secondary indexes and a planner in front, so callers stop hand-rolling loops. Module go-idxplan, package idxplan, this repo. Stdlib only. Split it like a real package — heap, index implementations, planner, executor in separate files, not one blob.

Records are map[string]string. NewHeap() gives the store: Insert(rec) uint64 assigns ids from 1, monotonically, never reusing a deleted id; Get(id) (map[string]string, bool); Update(id, rec) error replaces the whole record; Delete(id) error; Len() int. Update/Delete on a missing id return ErrNotFound (errors.Is-able). The heap owns its data: maps passed in or handed out are copies, callers scribbling on them must not corrupt the store.

Indexes: CreateHashIndex(name, field) and CreateOrderedIndex(name, field) — duplicate names are an error naming the offender; creating an index on a populated heap backfills it; records that lack the field simply aren't in that index. Every Insert/Update/Delete keeps ALL indexes exact — stale entries after an update are precisely the bug this replaces.

Queries: Eq(field, value) and Range(field, lo, hi *string) with lo <= value < hi in plain byte order, nil meaning unbounded (store zero-padded numbers if you want numeric ranges). Explain(q) (Plan, error) returns exactly what Search would do, without executing. Plan is the pinned explain output:

    type Plan struct {
        Kind  string  // "hash" | "ordered" | "scan"
        Index string  // chosen index name, "" for a scan
        Field string
        Eq    *string // set for Eq queries
        Lo    *string // set for Range queries (nil = unbounded)
        Hi    *string
    }

Planner rules: Eq prefers a hash index on the field, falls back to an ordered index, else scan. Range can only use an ordered index (hash can't do ranges), else scan. If several eligible indexes of the winning kind cover the field, pick the lexicographically smallest name so plans are stable. Search(q) ([]uint64, error) executes the plan and always returns ids ascending. A query with an empty field errors from both. ScanCount() int reports how many Searches had to walk the whole heap — that's our regression alarm for 'the planner stopped using indexes'. Explain must not bump it.

heap_test.go and plan_test.go are the acceptance contract — don't touch them; go test -race -timeout 30s ./... must pass.
